g4_revenue_trend.description: Revenue rose from $14.0B in 2017 to $15.0B in 2018 to $16.7B in 2019. Advisory and brokerage demand was not in visible structural decline at the decision point.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: For a broker and consultancy, gross margin is less meaningful than operating conversion. Public filings showed stable underlying margin quality rather than a two-year deterioration.

g7_moat.description: Marsh had a real moat through client relationships, global placement scale, data, and sticky corporate risk-management workflows. Large commercial clients do not casually rip out their broker, benefits, or consulting relationships.

quality_multiple_note.summary: Marsh traded on a low earnings yield because the market prized the durability of its client relationships and advisory embedment.
quality_multiple_note.reason_for_low_yield: This was a quality multiple on a real tollbooth-like service franchise, not a low-quality story stock.
quality_multiple_note.decision_impact: The note records that g6 is harsh on this kind of business, but the archived conclusion remains PASS for comparability.

management_veto.summary: There was no capital-allocation or integrity veto. The only issue was paying a premium valuation for a high-quality franchise.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — similarly excellent, but also priced too richly for the framework.

control_groups.company: Willis Towers Watson Public Limited Company
control_groups.conclusion: PASS — same broad category, but weaker quality and messier execution than Marsh.

buffett_action: Berkshire reported 4,267,825 Marsh & McLennan shares worth about $499.3M at 2020-12-31 in its 13F-HR.
actual_result: The stake actually increased to 5.29M shares by 2021-03-31, but Berkshire had cut it to just 404,911 shares by 2021-12-31 and kept only that token amount at 2022-12-31. It never became a core Berkshire position.
key_insight: Some wonderful service tollbooths are simply too expensive for the framework, especially when Berkshire itself treats them more like opportunistic positions than permanent pillars.
